elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we study the componentwise linearity of symbolic powers of edge ideals.
elements.sent-2411-002: We propose the conjecture that all symbolic powers of the edge ideal of a cochordal graph are componentwise linear.
elements.sent-2411-003: This conjecture is verified for some families of cochordal graphs, including complements of block graphs and complements of proper interval graphs.
elements.sent-2411-004: As a corollary, Minh's conjecture is established for such families.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, we show that $I(G)^{(2)}$ is componentwise linear, for any cochordal graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Let $S=K[x_1,\ldots,x_n]$ be the standard graded polynomial ring over a field $K$.
elements.sent-2411-007: By a classical result of Cutkosky, Herzog and Trung \cite{CHT , and independently Kodiyalam \cite{K , the regularity of powers of a graded ideal $I\subset S$ is an eventually linear function.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: This had a great impact on the study of homological invariants of powers of graded ideals.
elements.sent-2411-009: A prominent trend in commutative algebra is to explicitly determine this function for combinatorially defined monomial ideals.
elements.sent-2411-010: For instance, consider a finite simple graph $G$ with the vertex set $V(G)=\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}$ and edge set $E(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-011: The edge ideal of $G$ is the squarefree monomial ideal of the polynomial ring $S$ defined as $I(G)=(x_ix_j:\, \{x_i,x_j\}\in E(G))$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Then, there exist integers $k_0>0$ and $c\ge0$ such that $\reg\,I(G)^k=2k+c$ for all $k\ge k_0$.
elements.sent-2411-013: Determining the integers $k_0$ and $c$ in terms of combinatorics of $G$ is a problem of great interest.
elements.sent-2411-014: In recent years, the study of symbolic powers of monomial ideals, and in particular, edge ideals, has also gained significant attention, see for instance \cite{BC,DHNT,GHOS,JK,KKS,MNPTV,MTr,MVu,F1,F3,F2 and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-015: While the regularity of symbolic powers of monomial ideals is known to be a quasi-linear function~\cite[Corollary 3.3]{HHT , its precise behavior remains mysterious.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Minh raised the following \begin{Conjecture Let $I(G)$ be the edge ideal of a simple graph $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Thus far, Conjecture \ref{ConjA has been proved when $k=2,3$ for any graph~\cite{MNPTV , and for few families of graphs, including bipartite graphs~\cite{SVV , chordal graphs~\cite{F2 , unicyclic graphs~\cite{F1 and Cameron-Walker graphs~\cite{F3 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Therefore, if Conjecture \ref{ConjA is true, then $\reg\,I(G)^{(k)}\ge2k$ for all $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This naturally leads to the question: for which graphs $G$ does the equality $\reg\,I(G)^{(k) =2k$ hold for all $k$? In particular, in this case $I(G)$ itself must have linear resolution.}
elements.sent-2411-023: By Fröberg's seminal work \cite{Froberg88 , this is equivalent to $G$ being a cochordal graph, meaning that the complementary graph $G^c$ of $G$ is chordal.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, an intriguing result of Herzog, Hibi and Zheng \cite{HHZ establishes that if $I(G)$ has linear resolution, then $I(G)^k$ has linear resolution for all $k\ge1$.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This is further equivalent to $I(G)^k$ having linear quotients for all $k\ge1$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Now, let $I(G)$ be an edge ideal with linear resolution.
elements.sent-2411-027: In general, the symbolic powers $I(G)^{(k)}$ are not equigenerated, so one cannot expect that they have linear resolution, like in the case of the ordinary powers.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, based on several computational evidence, we expect that each graded component of $I(G)^{(k)}$ has linear resolution, i.e., $I(G)^{(k)}$ is componentwise linear.
elements.sent-2411-029: Componentwise linear ideals were introduced in \cite{HH99 by Herzog and Hibi, as those homogeneous ideals $I\subset S$ whose all graded components $I_{\langle j\rangle}$ have linear resolution.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Recall that the $j^{th}$ graded component of $I$ is the ideal generated by all homogeneous elements of degree $j$ belonging to $I$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Componentwise linear ideals are characterized by the remarkable property that their graded Betti numbers are equal to those of their generic initial ideals~\cite{AHH .}
elements.sent-2411-032: These aforementioned considerations on edge ideals of cochordal graphs led us to formulate the following \begin{Conjecture Let $I(G)$ be the edge ideal of a simple graph $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Then $I(G)^{(k)}$ is componentwise linear for all $k\ge1$.
elements.sent-2411-035: Since the highest generating degree of a componentwise linear ideal $I$ is equal to $\reg\,I$ \cite[Corollary 8.2.14]{HHBook , if Conjecture \ref{ConjB is true, then Conjecture \ref{ConjA is true for any cochordal graph $G$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our main goal in this paper is to address Conjectures \ref{ConjA and \ref{ConjB .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In Section \ref{sec1 , using a description of the symbolic Rees algebra of the edge ideal of a perfect graph $G$ due to Villarreal \cite{Vil , we determine in Theorem \ref{Thm:perfect the generating degrees of $I(G)^{(m)}$ for any perfect graph $G$ and any positive integer $m$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: In particular, it turns out that the highest degree of a minimal generator of $I(G)^{(m)}$ is $2m$, and if $G$ is cochordal then $\reg\,I(G)^{(m)}\ge\reg\,I(G)^{m}$ (see Corollary \ref{inequalityreg ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, we obtain an explicit formula for the Waldschmidt constant of the edge ideal of a perfect graph, recovering a result of Bocci {\it et al. \cite[Theorem 6.7(i)]{BC .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section \ref{sec2 , Conjecture \ref{ConjB is proved for the graphs whose complements are one of the following graphs:.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Indeed, we prove a more general result.
elements.sent-2411-043: Let $G$ be one of the graphs in (a), (b) or (c), and let $\Ass\,I(G)=\{P_1,\dots,P_m\}$ be the set of associated primes of $I(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Theorem \ref{Thm:forestComLinear shows that $\bigcap_{i=1 ^mP_i^{k_i}$ is componentwise linear for any positive integers $k_1,\ldots,k_m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: In the literature, such ideals are called intersection of Veronese ideals, and their componentwise linearity was first studied by Francisco and Van Tuyl \cite{FVT .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Another family of such ideals with the componentwise linear property was given in \cite[Theorem 2.4]{MM .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Theorem \ref{Thm:forestComLinear presents several new such families.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We expect that for any cochordal graph $G$, the ideal $\bigcap_{P\in\Ass\,I(G) P^{k_P}$ is componentwise linear for any positive integers $k_P$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: A monomial ideal $I\subset S$ has componentwise linear quotients if $I_{\langle j\rangle}$ has linear quotients for all $j$.
elements.sent-2411-052: To prove Theorem \ref{Thm:forestComLinear , we show that $I(G)^{(k)}$ has componentwise linear quotients.}
elements.sent-2411-053: A conjecture by Soleyman Jahan and Zheng \cite{SJZ states that if $I$ has componentwise linear quotients, then $I$ has linear quotients.}
elements.sent-2411-054: This conjecture is widely open, and has been solved only in some special cases \cite{BQ1,BQ2,Fic0 .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Given these facts, along with the proof of Theorem \ref{Thm:forestComLinear , we expect that the following more general statement than Conjecture \ref{ConjB holds true.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Then $I(G)^{(k)}$ has linear quotients for all $k\ge1$.
elements.sent-2411-057: The proof is based on Theorem \ref{Thm:perfect (a) and some splittings of the $t$-clique ideals of $G$.}
elements.section-2411-002: The generating degrees of symbolic powers of edge ideals of perfect graphs
elements.sent-2411-058: In this section, the generating degrees of $I(G)^{(m)}$ are studied, when $G$ is a perfect graph.
elements.sent-2411-059: As a result, we derive an inequality in Conjecture~\ref{ConjA for cochordal graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-060: We begin the discussion with some definitions and notation.
elements.sent-2411-061: Throughout, $G$ is a finite simple graph.
elements.sent-2411-062: The vertex set and the edge set of $G$ are denoted by $V(G)$ and $E(G)$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-063: A graph $G$ is called {\em chordal if it has no induced cycles of length $r>3$, and $G$ is called {\em cochordal , if the complementary graph $G^c$ of $G$ is chordal.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Here, $G^c$ is the graph with the same vertex set as $G$ whose edges are the non-edges of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-065: A graph $G$ is called a {\em perfect graph, if $G$ and $G^c$ do not contain induced odd cycles of length $r>3$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The family of perfect graphs contains for instance the families of bipartite graphs, weakly chordal graphs (and in particular chordal graphs and cochordal graphs) and comparability graphs of posets.
elements.sent-2411-067: For a subset $A\subseteq V(G)$, the induced subgraph of $G$ on $A$ is denoted by $G[A]$.
elements.sent-2411-068: A {\em clique of $G$ is a subset $C\subseteq V(G)$ such that the induced subgraph $G[C]$ is a complete graph.}
elements.sent-2411-069: A clique of size $r$ is called an {\em $r$-clique .}
elements.sent-2411-070: The maximum cardinality of the cliques of $G$ is denoted by $\omega(G)$ and is called the {\em clique number of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Villarreal in~\cite[Corollary 3.3]{Vil gave a description for the symbolic Rees algebra \begin{equation}R_s(I(G))=S\oplus I(G)^{(1)}t\oplus \cdots \oplus I(G)^{(i)}t^i\oplus\cdots\subseteq S\end{equation} of $I(G)$, when $G$ is a perfect graph in terms of the cliques of $G$, as follows: \begin{equation}\label{terriblesossage} \mathcal{R}_s(I(G))=K[x_Ft^r: F is an $(r+1)$-clique of $G$],\end{equation} where $x_F=\prod_{x_i\in F x_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: For a positive integer $r$, the $r$-clique ideal of $G$ was defined in~\cite{Mor as \begin{equation}K_r(G)=(x_F: F is an $r$-clique of $G$).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-074: So excluding this case, in the following theorem we assume that $\omega(G)\geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-076: This is equivalent to $s_1+\dots+s_j=m+j$ and $2\le s_i\le \omega$ for all $i$, since $K_{s_i (G)=(0)$ for $s_i>\omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Moreover, from the inequalities $s_i\geq 2$, we obtain $2j\leq \sum_{i=1 ^j s_i=m+j$ and hence $j\le m$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Let $q=\lceil m/(\omega-1)\rceil$, and $q\le j\le m$ be an integer.
elements.sent-2411-079: We need to show that $I(G)^{(m)}$ has a minimal generator of degree $m+j$.
elements.sent-2411-080: First we claim that there exist integers $s_1,\dots,s_j$ such that $2\le s_i\le \omega$ for all $i$, and $s_1+\dots+s_j=m+j$.
elements.sent-2411-081: We prove this by induction on $j$.
elements.sent-2411-082: The first step of induction is $j=q$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Now, assume that $\omega-1$ does not divide $m$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Then $m=(\omega-1)(q-1)+r$, where $0<r<\omega-1$, and so $m+q=(q-1)\omega+r+1=s_1+\dots+s_q$, where $s_i=\omega$ for $1\le i\le q-1$ and $s_q=r+1$ with $2\leq s_q<\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-087: So the claim is proved for $j=q$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Now, let $j$ be an integer with $q<j\le m$, and assume inductively that there exist integers $s_1,\dots,s_{j-1}$, with $2\le s_i\le \omega$, such that $\sum_{i=1 ^{j-1 s_i=m+(j-1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Since $j\le m$, there exist $1\le i\le j-1$ such that $s_i>2$.
elements.sent-2411-091: This implies that $j\leq m=j-1$, which is a contradiction.
elements.sent-2411-093: Then \begin{equation}m+j=\sum_{i=1}^{j-1}s_i+1=\sum_{i=1}^{j-2}s_i+(s_{j-1}-1)+2=\sum_{i=1}^j s'_i,\end{equation} where $s'_i=s_i$ for $1\le i\le j-2$, $s'_{j-1 =s_{j-1 -1$ and $s'_j=2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: We have $2\le s'_i\le\omega$ for all $1\le i\le j$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Next, let $q\leq j\leq m$ be an integer.
elements.sent-2411-097: We present a monomial $f$ of degree $m+j$ and show that it is a minimal generator of $I(G)^{(m)}$.

elements.sent-2411-100: Without loss of generality, we may assume that $u=\prod_{i=1 ^{s_j x_i$.}}

elements.sent-2411-114: Moreover, since $a_i=0$ for $i>s_j$ and $a_i=p$, for any $s_{j-p +1\leq i\leq s_{j-p+1}$, where $1\leq p\leq j$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:j'2} \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=s_{j-j'+1}+1}^{n}b_i\ \le\ \sum_{i=s_{j-j'+1}+1}^{n}a_i \le\ (j'-1)(s_{j-j'+2}-s_{j-j'+1})+\cdots+3(s_{j-2}-s_{j-3}) \phantom{aaaa}+2(s_{j-1}-s_{j-2})+(s_j-s_{j-1}) =\ \sum_{\ell=1}^{j'-1}\ell(s_{j-\ell+1}-s_{j-\ell}). \end{aligned}\end{equation} We have \begin{equation}\label{eq:j'3} \sum_{\ell=1}^{j'-1}\ell(s_{j-\ell+1}-s_{j-\ell})=\sum_{\ell=0}^{j'-2}(\ell+1)s_{j-\ell}-\sum_{\ell=1}^{j'-1}\ell s_{j-\ell}=\sum_{\ell=0}^{j'-2}s_{j-\ell}-(j'-1)s_{j-j'+1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-115: From equations (\ref{eq:j'1 ), (\ref{eq:j'2 ) and (\ref{eq:j'3 ), we see that \begin{equation}m+j'=\sum_{i=1}^{j'} s'_i\ \leq\ j's_{j-j'+1}+ \sum_{\ell=0}^{j'-2}s_{j-\ell}-(j'-1)s_{j-j'+1}=s_j+s_{j-1}+\cdots+s_{j-j'+1}.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.section-2411-003: Componentwise linearity of symbolic powers of edge ideals
elements.sent-2411-137: This section focuses on resolving Conjecture \ref{ConjB for several classes of graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-138: To set the stage for the main result, we provide a brief review of some key concepts.
elements.sent-2411-139: A vertex $x$ of a graph $G$ is called a {\em simplicial vertex of $G$ if its (open) neighborhood $N_G(x)=\{y\in V(G): \{x,y\}\in E(G)\}$ is a clique of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-140: A {\em perfect elimination ordering of $G$ is an ordering $x_1>\cdots>x_n$ on the vertex set of $G$ such that $x_i$ is a simplicial vertex of the induced subgraph $G_i=G[\{x_i,x_{i+1 ,\ldots,x_n\}]$ for all $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: By a classical result due to Dirac~\cite{Dirac , $G$ is a chordal graph, if and only if, $G$ has a perfect elimination ordering.}
elements.sent-2411-142: A graph $G$ is called a proper interval graph, if there exists a labeling $\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}$ on the vertex set of $G$ such that for any $i<j<k$, $\{x_i,x_k\}\in E(G)$ implies that $\{x_i,x_j\}\in E(G)$ and $\{x_j,x_k\}\in E(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-143: It can be seen that $x_1>\ldots>x_n$ is a perfect elimination ordering of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-144: Hence, any proper interval graph is chordal.
elements.sent-2411-145: A {\em cut vertex of a connected graph $G$ is a vertex $x\in V(G)$ such that $G\setminus x$ is not connected.}
elements.sent-2411-146: A block of a graph $G$ is a maximal induced subgraph $B$ of $G$ with the property that it is connected and contains no cut vertex.
elements.sent-2411-147: A graph $G$ is called a block graph if all of its blocks are cliques of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-148: For instance, any forest is a block graph.
elements.sent-2411-149: In the sequel we use the following characterization of block graphs, for which we refer to~\cite{Ho .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Given a non-empty subset $A$ of $[n]$ and a monomial $u=x_1^{a_1}\cdots x_n^{a_n}\in S$, we set $P_A=(x_i:i\in A)$ and $u_A=\prod_{i\in A x_i^{a_i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The following simple lemma will be used several times.
elements.sent-2411-153: Then, $u\in\bigcap_{i=1 ^mP_{[n]\setminus A_i ^{k_i}$ if and only if \begin{equation}\deg(u_{A_i})\le d-k_i,\,\,\,\,\,for all\,\,1\le i\le m.\end{equation}. \end{Lemma.}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: We have $u\in P_{[n]\setminus A_i ^{k_i}$ if and only if $ \sum_{j\in[n]\setminus A_i a_j\ge k_i.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Recall that an independent set of $G$ is a subset $A$ of $V(G)$ such that no two vertices of $A$ are adjacent in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-156: The set of all independent sets of $G$ is a simplicial complex $\Delta_G$, called the independence complex of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-157: A {\em vertex cover of $G$ is a subset $C\subseteq V(G)$ which intersects each edge of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-158: A minimal set with such property is called a {\em minimal vertex cover of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-159: As customary, if $\Delta$ is a simplicial complex, we denote by $\mathcal{F (\Delta)$ the set consisting of the facets of $\Delta$.. Then \begin{equation}I(G)^{(k)}\ =\ \bigcap_{A\inF(\Delta_G)}P_{[n]\setminus A}^k.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Indeed, $P_C\in\Ass\,I(G)$ if and only if $C$ is a minimal vertex cover of $G$, which means that $C=[n]\setminus A$ for a maximal independent set $A\in\mathcal{F (\Delta_G)$.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Notice that any maximal independent set $A\in\mathcal{F (\Delta_G)$ is a maximal clique of $G^c$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: We will use this basic fact several times.
elements.sent-2411-163: A homogeneous ideal $I\subset S$ is called {\em componentwise linear , if the ideal \begin{equation}I_{\langle d\rangle}=(f\in I, \, f is homogeneous of degree d)\end{equation} has linear resolution for any positive integer $d$.}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}I_{\langle d\rangle}=(f\in I, \, f is homogeneous of degree d)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-164: A useful approach to show that an ideal is componentwise linear is to show that it has (componentwise) linear quotients.
elements.sent-2411-165: Indeed, ideals with (componentwise) linear quotients are componentwise linear \cite[Theorem 8.2.15]{HHBook .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Recall that a monomial ideal $I$ has {\em linear quotients if the minimal generators of $I$ can be ordered as $u_1,\ldots,u_s$ such that for each $i = 2,\ldots,s$, the ideal $(u_1,\ldots,u_{i-1 ):(u_i)$ is generated by variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-167: In the following, for two monomials $u$ and $v$, we set $u:v=u/\gcd(u,v)$.
elements.sent-2411-170: Complement of a proper interval graph,.
elements.sent-2411-171: Then $\bigcap_{A\in\mathcal{F (\Delta_G) P_{[n]\setminus A ^{k_A $ is componentwise linear, for any positive integers $k_A$.}}}}
